video_title: Data is Back: MongoDB, Databricks, Snowflake
gist: MongoDB CEO CJ Desai argues that AI's model boom is driving an unexpected data explosion that benefits operational databases, and that hyperscaler capacity shortages plus data-sovereignty demands are fueling a genuine comeback of on-prem and enterprise data centers. He details MongoDB's three AI customer classes (frontier labs, AI-native startups, enterprises) and lessons on the messy, fast-changing agentic architecture stacks enterprises are building.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are running out of capacity and turning away even top customers: a Fortune 100 company in Texas that is a top-50 customer of one hyperscaler was told there was no capacity for additional cloud and AI workloads, pushing it to reverse a data-center decommissioning and run workloads on-prem instead.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals hyperscaler capacity is a binding constraint even for large customers, supporting demand for on-prem/data-center buildout and multicloud database vendors.
insights.quote: the hyperscaler said sorry we don't have a capacity and they are one of the top 50 customers for that hyperscaler

insights.claim: The 2010-2020 'everything moves to public cloud' consensus is reversing: capacity shortages and the need to keep proprietary data on-premises are driving data-center and on-prem demand back up, contradicting the assumption that all Fortune 500 workloads simply migrate to hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: A structural bullish signal for on-prem infrastructure, data-center operators, and databases that run outside a single cloud.
insights.quote: data centers are back and nobody is now saying hey uh especially in the fortune 500 and most people get that wrong because everybody thought hey all the workloads are just going to move to this hyperscaler

insights.claim: Hyperscaler capacity denial is forcing enterprises into complex multicloud setups: a large US telecommunications company was refused additional regional capacity by one hyperscaler and had to sign with a second hyperscaler, now managing its application real estate across multiple clouds.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Regional capacity gaps push customers to multicloud, favoring cloud-agnostic infrastructure and adding operational complexity that vendors can monetize.
insights.quote: this hyperscaler refused to give them additional regional capacity that they needed. So they signed with the second hyperscaler

insights.claim: MongoDB's multicloud portability is now a purchasing driver for AI companies: a frontier lab chose MongoDB specifically because it runs across multiple clouds for resiliency, and labs running out of capacity in AWS want to shift MongoDB workloads to GCP on demand.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Cloud-portability is becoming a competitive moat as capacity scarcity forces workload migration between clouds.
insights.quote: I'm running out of capacity in AWS so I want to move some of MongoDB to GCP how do we make it easy for them

themes.theme: Data as the downstream AI winner

insights.claim: The AI model boom is a data-generation boom: tokens and agents create ever more data, making the data layer a non-obvious downstream beneficiary of AI even as attention stays fixed on the models themselves.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames operational data infrastructure (MongoDB) as a pick-and-shovel AI play distinct from the crowded model layer.
insights.quote: data is the unsung hero and data is back. You cannot create an AI application without a great data layer and your AI application is as good as your data

insights.claim: MongoDB serves three distinct AI customer classes: frontier labs (multiple, undisclosed use cases including inference, voice, video and image products), AI-native companies (vibe-coding platforms, robotics/physical AI), and enterprises building still-early agentic workloads.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Segmentation shows MongoDB's AI revenue spans the full stack, with enterprise agentic adoption as the still-untapped growth leg.
insights.quote: we have seen on the AI super cycle three classes of customers specifically to AI

insights.claim: ElevenLabs runs north of 50 million agents entirely on MongoDB, and AI-native startups including Emergent and Base44 (vibe-coding platforms) and Mistral.ai are built on MongoDB, evidencing fit for agentic workloads at scale.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Concrete scale reference for MongoDB's traction with high-growth AI-native customers; a proof point for the agentic-memory-layer thesis.
insights.quote: they have north of 50 million agents depending on when you look at it all running on MongoDB

insights.claim: Enterprise customer-facing agentic applications are still not running at scale — even at airlines and banks, users do not yet see many agents in production apps — indicating the agentic enterprise wave is still in the tinkering/prototyping phase.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Enterprise agentic deployment is earlier than headlines suggest; the inference/production ramp is a future catalyst, not a present one.
insights.quote: I have not seen end customer facing agentic applications run at scale

insights.claim: MongoDB frames frontier labs as its 'holy grail' customers because their user growth is near-vertical rather than linear, validating its architecture for storing unstructured data in real time and acting as an agent memory layer.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Ties MongoDB's growth to the steepest-growing segment in AI; frontier-lab adoption is a leading indicator to watch.
insights.quote: their scale and how fast they are growing is like not even linear in some of the cases when I see weekly active users or daily active users is almost literally a vertical uh spike

insights.claim: Data sovereignty is now the top infrastructure priority for regulated European enterprises: a large Paris-based customer told MongoDB it will not move workloads to public cloud at all, keeping everything on-prem because French regulation makes sovereignty the highest priority.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Regulatory sovereignty is a durable tailwind for on-prem and sovereign-AI deployments, especially in Europe.
insights.quote: We are not going to move our workloads to public cloud. We are going to run it on prem. Data sovereignty is highest priority. French regulations forces us to have that as the highest priority

insights.claim: Sovereignty concern is broadening beyond regulated industries — the Texas Fortune 100 customer moving workloads on-prem is not in a regulated industry, showing proprietary-data protection is becoming a general enterprise driver, echoing Alex Karp's public warnings about data caution when working with AI labs.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Proprietary-data protection as a mainstream (not just regulated) motive expands the addressable on-prem/sovereign market.
insights.quote: this is not a regulated industry customer they're just a large customer in Texas

insights.claim: MongoDB positions itself in a different category from Snowflake and Databricks: it is an operational/real-time OLTP document database (for credit-card transactions and other real-time workloads), whereas Snowflake and Databricks are analytical OLAP systems that answer business-analyst queries and are not real-time.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Clarifies MongoDB competes on the operational data layer, not analytics — relevant for sizing overlap and TAM against the analytics names.
insights.quote: MongoDB is a operational database or a real-time database... The category is called online transaction processing or OLTP

insights.claim: MongoDB integrates vector search and embeddings from Voyage AI natively so data can be stored, searched, and reasoned over in one system without separate pipelines, and claims 75% of the Fortune 100 plus leading AI-native startups run on it.
insights.speaker: sponsor read / CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Consolidated vector + operational store is MongoDB's differentiation pitch against stitching multiple tools; Fortune 100 penetration is a stated moat metric.
insights.quote: It's why 75% of the Fortune 100 and leading AI native startups run on MongoDB

insights.claim: MongoDB does not compete with data-labeling / RL companies and views them as complementary, while noting they are growing exponentially fast.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Removes a perceived competitive overlap and flags data-labeling as a separate fast-growing segment.

insights.claim: Enterprise agentic stacks are exploding in complexity: a large New York bank's agentic architecture diagram had roughly 55 boxes (varied LLMs, frameworks, databases, vector databases, embeddings), and half of them did not exist a year earlier, with the customer expecting even more boxes to be added.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Rapidly expanding agent stacks mean sustained tooling spend and integration complexity across observability, security, and guardrails.
insights.quote: this particular picture had I want to say 55 different boxes for the agentic architecture... Half of these boxes would have not been there

insights.claim: Enterprises need deterministic, auditable, human-in-the-loop agents for regulated decisions: a large insurance-company MongoDB customer requires state-by-state, bias-free, deterministic outputs with audit and human oversight before agents can quote or bind policies.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Determinism, auditability and compliance are gating requirements slowing regulated-industry agent deployment — a bottleneck and an opportunity for governance tooling.
insights.quote: they have to make sure these agents don't give something there is no bias involved and give a deterministic outcome uh and do you need human in the loop

insights.claim: There is no model standardization inside enterprises: for different use cases a single customer mixes domain-specific LLMs, open-source models, small language models, and closed models like OpenAI's, spanning open-to-closed and small-to-large horizontal-to-domain-specific.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Multi-model heterogeneity persists, favoring model-agnostic infrastructure and undercutting any single-model winner-take-all thesis.
insights.quote: there is no like standardization even when it comes to these models... it goes anywhere from open source to closed source small to large horizontal to domain specific based on the use cases

insights.claim: The open- vs closed-source model debate has no clear winner in practice: coding agents almost always use proprietary/closed models (Claude Code, Codex, Grok, Cursor options), while some domain-specific use cases pull open-source models from Hugging Face, leaving a persistently mixed world.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the 'open source is taking over' narrative; closed models still dominate high-value coding-agent workloads.
insights.quote: I have not seen a complete pivot towards open source or a complete pivot towards proprietary

insights.claim: A prediction-market customer (founder-CEO) nearly ran out of MongoDB capacity days before World Cup soccer demand peaked, texting CJ Desai specific terabyte needs to avoid going down — illustrating extreme, unplanned capacity spikes at fast-growing AI/real-time firms.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Event-driven demand spikes create acute infrastructure risk; vendors that solve elastic autoscaling capture stickier high-growth accounts.
insights.quote: it looks like we may be out of capacity in few days on MongoDB and the World Cup soccer is still couple of weeks away

insights.claim: AI-native companies refuse to hire expensive database administrators (historically the most expensive Oracle hires) and instead demand machine-based, self-managing, proactive autoscaling databases — reshaping infrastructure-software product requirements.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Autonomous DB operations become a must-win feature; labor substitution via automation is a product-roadmap priority for infra vendors.
insights.quote: these AI companies are saying we don't have time to hire these people. If we are using your software, it should have its own machine-based database administration

themes.theme: Pace of change & the forward-deployed-engineer shift

insights.claim: Every hyperscaler announced forward-deployed-engineer programs within roughly the last 8 weeks, effectively adopting Palantir's playbook, because customers going through rapid AI change need hands-on help.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: A visible industry-wide shift toward high-touch deployment services validates Palantir's model and signals a services layer growing atop AI infra.
insights.quote: over last 8 weeks lot of announcements from hyperscalers on forward deployed engineers they are using Palunteer's uh playbook

insights.claim: Because software now writes software, the incremental cost of innovation and the cost of experimentation have both collapsed, so companies pivot roadmaps faster than ever and 'this is the slowest it will ever be.'
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Falling cost of experimentation accelerates product churn and shortens planning horizons — a structural change in how software companies operate and compete.
insights.quote: this is the slowest I think it's it is going to get even faster

insights.claim: MongoDB's CEO is cautiously non-bearish on data centers in space, reasoning that if energy (not physical space) is AI's binding constraint, proximity to the sun for continuous solar energy makes it physically plausible, citing SpaceX offering capacity to Anthropic alongside terrestrial builds in Mississippi and Memphis.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: Reinforces energy-as-the-binding-constraint framing for AI compute; space data centers are a speculative long-dated optionality he rates as non-zero.
insights.quote: if truly energy is the constraint for the world of AI... how closer do you go to the sun to get as much sun as possible

insights.claim: MongoDB has become a talent farm that competitors actively poach: ElevenLabs' Mati and others target MongoDB salespeople (well-trained go-to-market) and engineers (strong on distributed systems and data), a signal of the company's talent density.
insights.speaker: CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)
insights.so_what: High talent density and poaching pressure is a soft indicator of organizational quality but also a retention risk to monitor.
insights.quote: everybody told me we should hire salespeople from MongoDB because you guys do really good job of training them
